title: Security+ SY0-701 — Section 18: Wireless Security
description: Jason Dion Security+ SY0-701 — Section 18: Wireless Security. Domain: Security Architecture (3.0).

questions.question: What improvement does WPA3 provide over WPA2?
answers: Stronger encryption with SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) and forward secrecy
Faster wireless speeds
Longer password requirements
Support for older devices
questions.explanation: WPA3 introduces SAE (replacing PSK handshake), providing stronger protection against offline dictionary attacks, plus individual data encryption per session (forward secrecy).

questions.question: What is an evil twin attack?
answers: A rogue access point mimicking a legitimate one to intercept wireless traffic
A denial of service attack on wireless networks
Stealing wireless passwords via brute force
Using two wireless cards simultaneously
questions.explanation: An evil twin is a fraudulent Wi-Fi access point that appears legitimate, tricking users into connecting so the attacker can intercept their traffic.

questions.question: Why is WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy) considered insecure?
answers: It uses weak RC4 encryption with static keys and can be cracked in minutes
It does not support encryption
It only works with older devices
It requires too much processing power
questions.explanation: WEP uses RC4 with short, static initialization vectors that can be intercepted and cracked using freely available tools, making it completely insecure.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 802.1X in wireless networks?
answers: Port-based network access control using RADIUS for enterprise authentication
Encrypting wireless traffic with AES
Providing wireless QoS
Managing wireless channel selection
questions.explanation: 802.1X provides port-based access control requiring authenticated credentials (via RADIUS) before network access is granted — used in WPA2/WPA3-Enterprise deployments.

questions.question: What is a rogue access point?
answers: An unauthorized wireless access point connected to the corporate network
A misconfigured official access point
An access point with outdated firmware
An access point with weak encryption
questions.explanation: A rogue AP is an unauthorized wireless access point installed on a network without approval, creating a security gap that attackers can exploit.

questions.question: What is a wireless jamming attack?
answers: Flooding the wireless spectrum with interference to cause denial of service
Intercepting wireless communications
Cracking wireless encryption keys
Spoofing MAC addresses on wireless networks
questions.explanation: Wireless jamming involves transmitting radio frequency interference to disrupt legitimate wireless communications, effectively causing a denial-of-service condition.

questions.question: What is bluejacking?
answers: Sending unsolicited messages to Bluetooth-enabled devices
Hijacking a Bluetooth connection to steal data
Using Bluetooth to track a device's location
Disabling a device's Bluetooth functionality
questions.explanation: Bluejacking involves sending unsolicited messages via Bluetooth to nearby devices. It's more of a nuisance than a serious threat, but demonstrates Bluetooth discovery exposure.

questions.question: What is bluesnarfing?
answers: Unauthorized access to information on a Bluetooth device
Sending spam messages via Bluetooth
Disabling a device's Bluetooth
Tracking a person using Bluetooth
questions.explanation: Bluesnarfing is an attack that exploits Bluetooth vulnerabilities to gain unauthorized access to data (contacts, messages, files) on a target device.

questions.question: Does hiding an SSID provide strong wireless security?
answers: No — SSID hiding is security through obscurity and easily bypassed with wireless scanners
Yes — hidden SSIDs cannot be detected
Yes — hidden SSIDs prevent all unauthorized connections
No — it makes the network slower
questions.explanation: SSID hiding is only security through obscurity. Tools like Kismet can easily detect hidden SSIDs by capturing probe requests. It should not be relied upon as a security control.

questions.question: What is a captive portal?
answers: A web page requiring authentication or agreement before granting internet access on a wireless network
A firewall portal for wireless traffic
A portal for configuring access points
A secure VPN gateway for wireless users
questions.explanation: Captive portals intercept wireless users and redirect them to a login or acceptance page before granting internet access — commonly used in hotels and airports.

questions.question: Why should WPS (Wi-Fi Protected Setup) be disabled?
answers: The WPS PIN can be brute-forced in hours, bypassing WPA2/WPA3 encryption
WPS slows down wireless speeds
WPS does not support modern encryption
WPS is only for older devices
questions.explanation: WPS uses an 8-digit PIN with a design flaw that effectively reduces it to 11,000 guesses. The Reaver tool can crack WPS in hours, compromising network security.

questions.question: Which encryption protocol should be used with WPA2 for best security?
answers: CCMP/AES — TKIP is deprecated and has known vulnerabilities
TKIP — it is the latest encryption standard
RC4 — it provides the fastest encryption
DES — it is the most widely supported
questions.explanation: CCMP (Counter Mode CBC-MAC Protocol) using AES is the secure choice for WPA2. TKIP was a temporary fix for WEP and has known vulnerabilities.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a wireless site survey?
answers: To map signal coverage, identify dead zones, and detect rogue access points
To configure access point channels
To test internet bandwidth
To update access point firmware
questions.explanation: A wireless site survey assesses the physical environment to optimize AP placement, coverage, and security — also used to detect unauthorized or rogue access points.

questions.question: What security risk is associated with NFC (Near Field Communication)?
answers: Eavesdropping and relay attacks on short-range transactions
Long-range interception of communications
Vulnerability to denial-of-service attacks
Interference with other wireless protocols
questions.explanation: NFC operates at very short range (< 20cm), but attackers can use relay attacks or eavesdrop on NFC transactions using specialized equipment.

questions.question: What is a disassociation (deauthentication) attack in wireless security?
answers: Forcing clients to disconnect from an AP by sending spoofed deauth frames
Blocking all wireless traffic in an area
Hijacking an authenticated wireless session
Cracking WPA2 encryption offline
questions.explanation: A deauthentication attack exploits the unprotected management frames in 802.11 to force clients to disconnect, often used to capture WPA handshakes for offline cracking.
